channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed

item.title: Age verification will roll out for PlayStation in the UK and Ireland
item.content:encoded:
Sony has announced that age verification will be rolling out later this year for PlayStation users in both the UK and Ireland. Without age verification, PlayStation users will not be able to use any of the communication features, broadcasting features, and some in game features. You can see the announcement below.

“Age verification will be required later in 2026 for UK & Ireland adult accounts to access certain features. If you’re prompted, we recommend completing age verification to avoid interruptions later in 2026. We use age verification to deliver the best PlayStation experience for your age and to help protect our younger users.

“If you have not completed age verification, you can continue playing on PlayStation but communication features won’t be available until you do. To keep using communication, broadcasting, and certain in-game features without interruption, we recommend verifying your age now. Verify now or scan this QR code on your mobile device.”

The QR code mentioned above can be found here within the announcement and FAQ.

There are three different ways to verify your age to continue accessing the communication features. That includes a facial scan through third party company Yoti, using ID age verification with a passport, driving license, or national ID, or through mobile phone age verification. This last method checks your age that your mobile phone provider has on their records.

Source: PlayStation



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/04/20/age-verification-will-roll-out-for-playstation-in-the-uk-and-ireland-in-june/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Interview: Double Fine's Kiln Feels Surprisingly Like A MOBA
item.content:encoded:
Double Fine Productions has always been a studio creating odd games. It’s best known for Psychonauts, an excellent platformer that’s surprisingly one of the studio’s more straightforward games. Since then, Double Fine has created some weirdly charming adventures like Costume Quest, Massive Chalice, and Rad. Last year’s Keeper was the studio’s most abstract game yet, following a walking lighthouse and a bird as they travel through a surrealist post-apocalyptic environment. It also featured no spoken dialogue, instead using visual indicators to convey emotion and gameplay.

Double Fine’s upcoming project, Kiln, is continuing the studio’s reputation, as it’s a multiplayer brawler involving…pottery? We briefly played 30 minutes of Kiln and spoke with director Derek Brand about how the idea came to be and the game’s long development cycle.

Kiln started as a project under Double Fine’s Amnesia Fortnight 2017 internal game jam, and studio head Tim Schafer ultimately chose for it to go under quiet development. However, it’s been nine years since then, so why did it take so long? The answer is simple: Brand and his team were pulled into other projects while working on Kiln. Early development started on the game in 2019 after he had finished his work on Rad. Then, of course, COVID-19 hits in 2020.

“That was a little weird for a while, and then we really had to finish Psychonauts 2. So my team got pulled in to help finish it,” he explains. “When that was done and wrapped up, then we went back to Kiln. And that's also when we broke off and worked on Keeper.” It wasn’t until after Psychonauts 2 was done that the developers could start ramping up on other projects.

Kiln also originally had a slightly darker atmosphere with goofy-looking spirits, and Brand’s team always wanted a more playful tone. Eventually, they decided on the concept of pottery because of its broad appeal. You can see the remnants of the spirit concept while you’re waiting in Kiln’s hub area. You float around as a small spirit inhabiting big pieces of pottery in preparation for battle. Brand wanted to create a pottery power fantasy and welcome those who wouldn’t normally play games.

“We think there's a lot of potential for people who just like making pots,” Brand says. The team also added different cosmetic attachments to give players a balance on whether they want to decorate their pots. He continues, ”There are some very light-hearted and cute things. Then there's also some heavy metal spikes and more metal materials. So you can really go either way, if you want to.”

Before my hands-on time with Kiln, I took a one-hour pottery session, and I discovered how much force and precision I needed to craft a pot that looked even. You need to push the clay around, splash water on it so it doesn’t dry, and drive your elbows into your hips to stabilize yourself. It’s quite a lot of work! In Kiln, the process to actually create the pot is much easier. You just simply need to use the control sticks to push in the direction you want to mold your creation. Want a taller flask? Just move your control stick upwards.

Early on in development, parts of Kiln felt more like a pottery simulator, but players weren’t jiving with those aspects. “We had some really interesting tech that felt like you had to push the clay around, and it kind of fought you a little bit, and had to get really good over time to make anything interesting out of it,” Brand explains. “And ultimately, it was just really punishing, and people bounced off of that.”

As a result, the team stripped away many of the more complex and difficult sculpting mechanics, and started providing more assists. “We wanted to make sure that if you just held and moved the control stick, that you could make a cool shape,” he says. “So we really tried to find areas where we could reintroduce some of the more realistic aspects of the clay, but do that in a way that was more welcoming and more approachable.”

Depending on how you sculpt your clay and the size of it, your pot can fit into three typical size archetypes: small, medium, and large. They all come with different abilities and attacks, such as swinging a sword or running into enemies. Kiln’s primary multiplayer mode is called “Quench,” where teams of 4vs4 must collect water to put out the kiln fire on the opponent’s side of the field.

While Double Fine defines Kiln as a brawler, I was surprised at how much the matches felt like playing a MOBA. The arena was filled with twists and turns. Navigating them was like defending a lane like in League of Legends. I also wiped out other players while they were trying to transport water, and vice versa. The respawn times allowed players on both sides to regroup and strategize.

Brand notes that the team has actually heard the MOBA comparisons, since the objective of Quench is to attack the enemy base.“We didn't necessarily look at MOBAs, but I think there's a lot of validity in that comparison,” he explains. “Really, we see [Quench] as this is our main game mode right now. We want to expand their game modes, but this is just one game mode that this one feels particularly similar to MOBA objectives.” However, Kiln is still a brawler at heart, and there are plans for future game modes with a larger variety of objectives.

Kiln will be on Xbox Game Pass when it launches on April 23 for PC, PS5, and Xbox Series X|S. Brand wants the game to be a comfortable and accessible experience that you don’t have to sink massive amounts of time into, unlike other online games that demand that you act like it’s a part-time job.

“I see a game that's really great for Game Pass, and we want a bunch of players to play it. It's something you can dive into and not commit a ton of time to. I just want to dive in and make some pottery, fight, and then collect a few cool new things that I can put on my future pots,” he explains. “I think there’s a place for that kind of game.”




https://www.mmorpg.com/interviews/interview-double-fines-kiln-feels-surprisingly-like-a-moba-2000137852
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Cthulhu: The Cosmic Abyss – Chapter 1 Guide
item.content:encoded:
Cthulhu: The Cosmic Abyss is the latest Lovecraftian horror game to hit the market, but beyond its horror elements, it is first and foremost a puzzle game.

Of course, the fun in every puzzle game is solving the puzzle that the developers have put in front of you, but it's also understandable if you need a hint or two. This guide, and the other six on their way, will take you through each chapter of Cthulhu: The Cosmic Abyss.

For more on Cthulhu: The Cosmic Abyss, check out my review. You can also check out our PC optimization guide to get the best performance out of your machine.

Cthulhu: The Cosmic Abyss - Chapter 1 Guide

The first chapter of Cthulhu: The Cosmic Abyss is designed to introduce you to the gameplay loop the game will run through for the rest of its six chapters, beginning with how you'll be trying to pick up, manipulate, and move through the environment to reach new areas, discover new clues, and use what's in the material world to impact the more occult nature of R'lyeh.

Once you arrive at Mei's place, you'll head left at the door to find a way inside so you can unlock the front door for Elsa. You'll head left from the front door and move a plant out of the way in order to crouch under some debris and find a path inside the house to the second floor.

Once you're inside, head to the first room on your right before going downstairs to find Mei's phone. You'll also find other objects inside Mei's room you can examine on either bedside table. Picking them up and examining them are part of the game's opening tutorials, so take your time to learn the flow of manipulating objects.

After letting Elsa in, you'll be tasked to look for more information about Mei. To your right, in the room where Elsa is digging into Mei's research tablet, you'll find a stone that you can pick up and analyze. This is where the game teaches you how to analyze clues to reveal Sonar frequencies, that will in turn help you discover more clues.

In the next room, you'll find a partially completed image made up of pages stuck to the wall. The pages required to finish the image are on the floor in front of the image and on the desk to your left. The final image is the third image in the gallery below, if you need a reference.

After completing the image and speaking to Elsa, the next part of the chapter kicks off when you head downstairs to the basement. Move towards the kitchen and turn left and you'll find a long black cord to follow that'll lead you downstairs. Once there, walk towards the door behind the stairs and a cutscene will begin.

It's worth noting that there are plenty of things to interact with along the way that'll prompt more dialogue from Noah, Elsa, and Key. There's also voice recordings from Mei to find, and notes of hers to read. You can and should interact with all of these, since the name of the game is always having as much information as possible. But, on the flip side of that is the fact that you don't need to solve every mystery that Cthulhu: The Cosmic Abyss throws at you. It's important to be able to parse which information is more world building and which information is actively helping you solve the task at hand.

Once you've been into the basement and discovered the organic matter, Elsa and Noah will deduce that it is some sort of portal, and you'll be tasked with finding a way to open it. You'll notice it bears some resemblance to the image you put together below. To open the portal, simply find the correct rocks and place each of them in the proper slots on either side. The second and third images in the gallery below are the correct rocks, for reference.

After you've opened the portal, a cutscene will play, after which you'll take your first steps into R'lyeh. Walk to your left, and another cutscene will eventually kick off and close out the chapter.

There are no upgrades or collectibles to find in this chapter, so taking all the time you want exploring is more about taking the time to learn as much as possible about the different investigation mechanics. There are more coming, and the game front-loads everything in one big shot between the first two chapters, so it's important to get as comfortable with each new mechanic as it comes.

About the author: David has been writing about videogames, technology, and culture since 2020, with a focus on reporting daily news across multiple publications, including GameDaily.Biz, GameSkinny, and PlayStation Universe before joining Wccftech in 2025. David started contributing as Canada/US reporter for Wccftech's gaming section in 2025. Besides being up-to-date on the industry's movements, he loves interviewing developers, reviewing games, and writing intricate essays about the symbolism and layered meanings to be found in rich narratives as he's done for publications like GamesIndustry.Biz, LostInCult, and others. Outside of games he loves movies, music, theatre, his hometown, and his family, though not necessarily in that order.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/how-to/cthulhu-the-cosmic-abyss-chapter-1-guide/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Debate-Lord Anime Nippon Sangoku Is 2026’s Must-Watch Thinking Man’s Show
item.content:encoded:
Anime has a storied history with what I like to call “the thinking man’s shows”—cerebral dramas built on double-crosses, galaxy-brained gambits, and verbal sleight-of-hand that reward viewers paying close attention. Its hieroglyphs are carved with greats: Legend of the Galactic Heroes, Code Geass, Death Note, Attack on Titan, The Apothecary Diaries, and, most recently, Orb: On the Movements of the Earth. Just when I resigned myself to 2026 passing without an heir worthy of that pantheon, Nippon Sangoku quietly released, bringing a fiery new animus to the category: debate bro energy. 

Nippon Sangoku: The Three Nations of the Crimson Sun, created by Ikka Matsuki and animated by Studio Kafka, is a political drama set against a backdrop of a collapsing Japan. According to its long-winded opening narration—which, mercifully, reappears every episode to handhold viewers through the minutiae of its political hellscape—Japan is in a very “those who don’t learn from history are doomed to repeat it” point in its life. Everything that can go wrong does: political corruption, acts of God, and nuclear warfare between the United States, Russia, the United Kingdom, Germany, and India thrash Japan’s economy so thoroughly that the Reiwa era (contemporary Japan, circa 2019) reverts back to something resembling the Meiji Era (1868 to 1912, when Japan started flirting with interacting with the Western world). 

In the aftermath, Japan fractures into three warring nations, each clawing for dominance through military might and puppy guarding resources. It’s basically what Alex Garland’s A24 movie Civil War was reaching for tonally if the film hadn’t been trying to be white folks’ City of God by way of HBO’s The Newsroom (and AI slop posters). 

At the center of its tale is Aoteru Misumi, a man the show delights in roasting as the most milquetoast of protagonists. He is a meek man with a basic bitch side-part that’d leave him lost in a crowd of extras, and he doesn't even have a nepo-baby pedigree to stand on. If anything, the most likable thing about him is his wife, Saki. Aoteru is also miles away from the kind of person you’d see at the front lines of Japan’s woke 2 like his street-smart wife, who also can’t help but voice her polite disappointment at how he keeps his gifts tucked away, treating them as passive talents rather than tools to get active with instilling real change in Japan. 

And Saki’s right, because what Aoteru does have is vital to her dreams of a restored Japan. Beneath all his self-effacing caution is a librarian with an historian’s grasp of humanity’s cycles of folly, ego, delusion, and breakthroughs. It’s also a mind, tragically, that’s smarter than he ever lets the world see, satisfied with living peacefully under the heel of Japan on the verge of a dictatorship than rock the boat with ideas of unification attained without force. 

Translation: Aoteru is your everyday moderate liberal man. 

That is, until tragedy strikes, a little too close to home, and he finally gets active as a legendary military strategist who aims to unify Japan on the strength of his sharp insight and persuasive gift of gab. 

Admittedly, an anime about a man going full Hasan Piker, verbally dismantling his opposition’s interlocutors, sounds wonderfully boring on paper in the big 2026. We’re living in un-parody-able times, after all. Yet, even in its early episodes, Nippon Sangoku has already become a standout for me. A huge part of that is because the show feels spiritually like Golden Kamuy, with an ensemble of larger-than-life weirdos whose unserious hijinks make you forget that Nippon Sangoku is, at its core, a political drama. And at the heart of that drama is the ideological clash between Aoteru and his sardonic, Sasuke-esque rival, Yoshitsune, whose “might is right” approach to unification is the polar opposite of Aoteru’s rhetoric-driven idealism. 

Another huge reason I’m enamored with Nippon Sangoku is how beautifully it’s animated. Without fail, the show turns what could’ve been a monotonous string of “debate‑bro destroys his ops” moments that’d win the internet on LiveStreamFails into something genuinely fascinating. And even as someone with only a college‑level grasp of Shōwa‑era history, I never felt lost in the political minutiae, proper‑noun avalanches, or policy deep‑cuts Aoteru fires off.

That’s thanks to the show’s clarity and expressive visual storytelling, which uses clever cues, asides, and metaphors to translate its double‑entendre political speak into something legible and lively. It eases viewers into its heady themes so gracefully that what could’ve played like a PBS history special disguised as anime instead becomes one of the most enthralling watches in an already packed season. It's the most fun I’ve had with an anime, much less a show, since Hulu’s Shōgun. 

Kafka really did their big one here. Given the production house’s work on The Ancient Magus’ Bride, one of Tatsuki Fujimoto’s stronger 17-26 one-shot anthology stories, and the third Mononoke film (not Studio Ghibli), Nippon Sangoku feels like a bravura showcase of the studio’s strengths. In one moment, the anime is a stirring epic with the fate of an entire nation at stake; in the next, it’s the most catch-you-off-guard funny misadventure committed to animation. All the while, it’s punctuated by an “oh shit” cliffhanger that’ll have your brain buzzing as the full tapestry of its dense world continues to take shape. 

The only downside is that Nippon Sangoku is a Prime Video exclusive show—a streaming platform where anime go to die in the algorithmic void without a peep of social media promotion. But with any luck, that obscurity, paired with it being one of the rare anime Hideo Kojima has spotlighted twice on his mostly Letterboxd-coded social media profiles, gives Nippon Sangoku the air of a hidden gem. And in its early goings, it absolutely earns that status.

Here’s hoping the show continues sharpening itself into a true thinking-man’s anime with teeth that gnash deeper with every new episode. 



https://aftermath.site/nippon-sangoku-anime-prime-video/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Ball X Pit The Shadow Update will release on April 27th
item.content:encoded:
Kenny Sun has revealed that the next free update for Ball X Pit will be launching on April 27th, and that update is the Shadow Update. That means players will get two new characters, 11 new balls, a brand new building, and four new passives. The full details have been given by Kenny Sun, and you can see the Ball X Pit The Shadow Update content below the teaser.

Ball X Pit The Shadow Update content details

  • The Tunneller is a scrappy miner whose shots don’t stop at the edge; they wrap around the screen and come back in from the other side, making just about every angle fair game.
  • The Tiptoer is a fragile but deadly ninja who stays undetected by enemies at close range at the cost of lower health and damage.
  • Venom stacks up with each hit, slowing targets and chipping away at them until enough buildup finally locks them in place with paralysis.
  • Time leans into area control, dropping a timesnare that freezes enemies caught in its range.
  • Erosion cuts straight through foes, with each hit shaving off a percentage of their current health, making it incredibly nasty against tougher targets.
  • Time Bomb adds a bit of delayed chaos, planting an explosive that goes off after a short wait.
  • Timestop does exactly what it sounds like, freezing absolutely everything on the field for a few seconds before going on cooldown.
  • Warp keeps things unpredictable, blinking to a random spot on the field upon every enemy hit while ramping up speed as it goes.
  • Sniper shots cut straight through enemies but burn out the moment they hit a wall or loop back, trading longevity for a boosted critical chance that rewards precise enemy line ups.
  • Heart Swallower gives each hit a certain chance to drain a bit of life and heal you for one while also weakening the target’s attack by a percentage, but remember each enemy can only be drained once.
  • Full Metal Rapier thrives on chaos with each ball scaling its damage based on how many baby balls and enemies are currently on the field.
  • Arrow of Fate flips defense into offense, making you immune to incoming projectiles and firing back a burst of baby balls whenever you’re hit.
  • Argent Stopwatch plays with momentum, starting balls off at 150% damage before shaving off 30% with every bounce, so timing and positioning matter more than ever.
  • Sword Breaker keeps things steady but scalable, lowering base damage to 75% while quietly stacking an extra 1% for every enemy in play.

  • Better start thinking of a spot to drop the Guildhall! This new building will allow players to re-roll previously chosen character upgrades.

Ball X Pit Review – Sphere factor

In our Ball X Pit review, Dom said, “Ball X Pit carries the spirit of an arcade classic, while imbuing it with modern roguelike ideas. It’s immediately one of the best indie games of the year.”

Source: Steam



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/04/20/ball-x-pit-the-shadow-update-will-release-on-april-27th/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Special Ruleset Vengeance Campaign is Back Today in The Elder Scrolls Online
item.content:encoded:
Things have been pretty busy in and around The Elder Scrolls Online in recent weeks. for the PvPers, the ESO team has got you, with a new Vengeance campaign starting today, with some important changes.

The Vengeance campaign is a special ruleset PvP mode in Cyrodiil that levels the playing field by limiting everyone eligible (levels 10 to 50) to build templates and normalized stats. When you enter, your character will change to match a certain build template for their class, and you'll get a backpack of supplies. The only things you take with you when the campaign ends are XP, gold, Alliance Points, and Champion Points. (The Champion System is disabled during the campaign.)

This time around, the campaign will run through April 27th and while this campaign is smaller in scope, there are a few changes to be aware of. First, some changes are intended to increase power for DPS players. Now, you can target more enemies using area of effect abilities, going up to six from a cap of three. These tweaks should speed things up and make them more aggressive. It should also impact healing, with more enemies targeted at once, you’lve got to be strategic and active if you’re the one doing the healing.

You can take part in this latest round via the Alliance War menu. More details on this month's campaign and Vengeance overall is over here in the announcement. 



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/the-special-ruleset-vengeance-campaign-is-back-today-in-the-elder-scrolls-online-2000137853
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Starfield Was The Best-Selling Game In The U.S. During The Week It Finally Arrived On PS5
item.content:encoded:
2023 Bethesda adventure Starfield finally made it to PlayStation 5 last week, alongside a couple of major content updates across all platforms. This combo propelled it to the top of Circana’s ranking of best-selling titles in the U.S. for its launch week, a spot it hasn’t sat in since its initial release. Not that the competition was especially stiff.

Starfield was the best-selling video game of the week in tracked physical & digital US full game dollar sales during the week ending April 11th.

It's the first time Starfield has led the weekly US best-selling titles chart since week ending Sept 2, 2023.

Source: Circana Retail Tracking Service

— Mat Piscatella (@matpiscatella.bsky.social) 2026-04-20T15:28:18.146Z

This comes from the above post by Circana senior director Mat Piscatella. Timing-wise, this is happening thanks to Starfield‘s launch two weeks ago on PlayStation, which coincided with the release of a sizeable free update, Free Lanes, as well as a paid expansion: Terran Armada. Free Lanes brings new weapons, quests, and a new vehicle to the game in an effort to improve space exploration, while Terran Armada is all about stopping the titular armada from attacking a bunch of planets, and includes even more new weapons and ships. Both updates are, per Todd Howard, intended for people who already love the game, as opposed to being a specific effort to draw in new players. “I think it’s the kind of thing where if you love Starfield, we think you’re going to love this,” he said in an interview with Kinda Funny.

All this likely means that the PS5 release on its own is pulling the majority of the weight here in raising Starfield’s sales numbers. It’s currently available on Xbox Game Pass, meaning we’re likely not going to see a massive surge of new full game purchases there. In total, Rhys Elliott from Alinea Analytics estimated it only sold about 140,000 copies during its PS5 launch week. Compare that to its initial launch, “the biggest Bethesda game launch of all time,” which reached over six million players on September 6 of 2023 and topped sales charts despite also being on Game Pass from the (space) jump.

It’s also prooobably worth pointing out that Starfield didn’t have a ton of hefty competition for sales during its PS5 launch week. Its PS5 release on April 7 was surrounded by the launch of Pokémon Champions on Nintendo Switch and…basically nothing else major. And Pokémon Champions is a free-to-start game. And it’s digital-only. Circana does not receive digital sales data from Nintendo, so there’s truly no telling how much better or worse Pokémon Champions did than Starfield.

According to Piscatella who spoke to Kotaku for this story, following Starfield on the U.S. sales charts at number two was MLB The Show 26 (with those numbers not including Xbox Series X or Nintendo Switch digital sales), followed by Call of Duty: Black Ops 7 at number three. Piscatella adds that this was Starfield’s fourth-biggest full game sales week in the U.S. since launch, being beaten only (unshockingly) by its first three weeks on the market. PlayStation 5 sales accounted for 95 percent of its new full game sales during that launch week (this excludes add-on content sales, like the expansion).

As far as actual player counts go, Piscatella tells us that Starfield jumped up to 26th from 140th in player count rankings on Steam for that PS5 and update launch week, and on Xbox it leaped from 56th to 18th. It debuted at 61st overall on PlayStation 5.

So is this news unquestionably a good thing for Starfield? Absolutely. It did well on PS5 in the U.S. and is clearly seeing a surge of interest now that it’s on a platform with a much larger install base. As Piscatella told us, “It was a solid debut for the PS5 version of the game. But, as always, we’ll have to see how its momentum carries over into future weeks.” Are we about to see a Starfield renaissance? Maybe? The rumor is that it might show up on the Nintendo Switch 2 later, which will likely give it another boost. But Howard also warned that we should not expect a Starfield 2.0 to draw in new fans. As he said in the same Kinda Funny interview: “If Starfield is something that didn’t connect with you right away, or you bounced off it, or found it boring in places, I don’t think this is going to change that fundamentally.” So if you’ve been waiting for it to come to your platform of choice before you go to space, there’s plenty to hope for. But if you just wished Starfield was something different…sorry, you might be stuck waiting for The Elder Scrolls 6. Whenever that is.



https://kotaku.com/starfield-best-selling-game-ps5-launch-2000689070
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: New Xbox CEO Asha Sharma “Would Love to Chat” With Former PlayStation Boss Shawn Layden
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot that has been said about Microsoft's new head of its gaming division and the Xbox platform, Asha Sharma, particularly with regard to the fact that she's coming into the world of video games from an outsider's perspective. But by the same token, she's also (at least publicly) made it abundantly clear she is trying to listen and learn from those already ingrained in the industry, and she just showed that same sentiment once again by reaching out to one of PlayStation's former bosses, Shawn Layden.

While Sharma represents the next era of leadership for one of the three major platform holders in video games, Layden represents a time long gone. As the president of Sony Computer Entertainment Japan and later the president and chief executive officer of Sony Interactive Entertainment between the PlayStation 3 through to the end of the PlayStation 4 generation, Layden saw the video game industry change dramatically between those generations and how they led towards what we have now, not just with regard to where PlayStation is, but the industry as a whole.

In the time since Layden stepped down in 2019, he's been known to share his thoughts on the industry as it's evolved. Which is what he was doing when Sharma publicly reached out with the invitation to chat. Spotted by GameReactor, in response to a post from Joost van Dreunen, a video game industry analyst whose comments we've covered previously, Layden, speaking of Xbox and Xbox Game Pass, said, "They are trying so hard to will this into health, despite unfavorable diagnostics and a grim prognosis. A clarifying post mortem would do the entire industry some good."

Sharma then responded to Layden, simply saying, "I would love to chat sometime."

The ramifications of a conversation between one current platform leader and a former leader could be massive, especially considering that Sharma is reportedly leading the rebrand of Xbox "personally," and that she is seemingly on the verge of making some major decisions for the company, like potentially decreasing the price of Game Pass.

It would be interesting to see Sharma take a point or two from Layden's perspective, even if he hasn't been at the head of the kinds of decisions Sharma has to face now for over five years. Of course, we'll likely never know what exactly the ramifications of a conversation between them are, if it happens at all. But it is positive to see Sharma so open to varying perspectives.

About the author: David has been writing about videogames, technology, and culture since 2020, with a focus on reporting daily news across multiple publications, including GameDaily.Biz, GameSkinny, and PlayStation Universe before joining Wccftech in 2025. David started contributing as Canada/US reporter for Wccftech's gaming section in 2025. Besides being up-to-date on the industry's movements, he loves interviewing developers, reviewing games, and writing intricate essays about the symbolism and layered meanings to be found in rich narratives as he's done for publications like GamesIndustry.Biz, LostInCult, and others. Outside of games he loves movies, music, theatre, his hometown, and his family, though not necessarily in that order.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/xbox-ceo-asha-sharma-would-love-to-chat-with-former-playstation-boss-shawn-layden/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: It’s Such A Relief To See A Cool Game Like Pragmata Succeed
item.content:encoded:
Just how different will Assassin’s Creed: Black Flag Resynced look on PS5 compared to how the previous version looked on PS4? Will Dragon Ball Xenoverse 3 be any easier to parse than its predecessor? And will Pragmata prove to be a beacon of hope, or the last gasp of an old way of making and selling games?

It’s your Morning Checkpoint for April 20, 2026, where we are excited to reveal our newest hire: Rebekah Valentine, formerly senior reporter at IGN. You might have already read her first post on the site covering the Elden Ring movie news earlier today. I think Kotaku is good, but it’s about to get even better.

Pragmata has already sold over 1 million copies

It’s nice to see Capcom’s new sci-fi, not a “sad dad” shooter being successful, if for no other reason than it proves big publishers can still take chances on new franchises with novel gameplay twists and unconventional premises and not get utterly clobbered by the grim hellscape that is the current video game market.

It’s not the outcome you’d expect for a game that didn’t click during its original reveal and was subsequently delayed “into oblivion.” It proves Capcom is on a hot streak at the moment, but also that you don’t have to be a gravity-distorting AAA blockbuster to have a chance at success, especially when publisher expectations are calibrated accordingly.

It no doubt helps that Pragmata came to every console platform and had a stellar Steam launch, and that it’s not a full-priced game. Whether Capcom chipped $10 off the box price because it’s a shorter linear adventure or to juice sales after so many years of development hell is anyone’s guess. But the result definitely reminded me of the PS2 days.

Bandai Namco’s secret new Dragon Ball game is Dragon Ball Xenoverse 3

The publisher made a bunch of announcements during the Battle Hour esports event in Los Angeles over the weekend, including that its next DBZ game is a sequel to the franchise, where players create their own character and explore a new variation of the traditional DBZ world/timeline. It’s sort of like an MMO that revolves around fighting-game-style 3D arena battles.

If you’re wondering why Bandai Namco keeps going back to this well, it’s probably because there are more microtransactions than you can shake a Power Pole at. The publisher even released a little guide earlier this year to help players figure out what they need to buy in what order to get the full experience.

Square Enix takes legal action against a fan’s videos

The company shared a notice on April 20 that it had taken legal action against someone posting videos harassing people involved in the making of Final Fantasy XIV. It claimed the user had published content which harmed the reputation of the company and employees involved in the MMO. Automaton reports an “amicable settlement with the user” has been reached.

Ubisoft reveals a tease for an announcement of a remake of a 13-year-old game

We’ll finally get our first good look at Assassin’s Creed Black Flag Resynced during a World Premiere Showcase on April 23 at 12:00 p.m. ET. It’s likely to be Ubisoft’s biggest release of 2026, and potentially the only major one, as games like the Splinter Cell remake and next Ghost Recon keep reportedly slipping internal milestones.

There’s also an ID@Xbox showcase this week

It’ll air on April 23 at 1:00 p.m. ET, immediately after the Ubisoft event. The showcase, which is hosted by IGN, promises updates on Mistfall Hunter, There Are No Ghosts at the Grand, Aphelion, Solo Leveling Arise Overdrive, and more.

Take-Two’s CEO takes a shot at AI and Elon Musk

Asked about the technology during a recent Semafor World Economy 2026 panel, Strauss Zelnick quipped that if AI was going to immediately take anyone’s job, it would probably be Musk’s. “He has unlimited financial resources and he has unlimited human resources and he has, apparently, an unlimited amount of ideas; he also knows his way around AI,” the executive in charge of Rockstar Games’ parent company said.

He continued, “The man works 20 hours a day, if AI were going to take anyone’s job wouldn’t it take his job, the richest guy on Earth? Wouldn’t that be like job number one for AI to take? Why is he so busy?” Before that, Zelnick made the point that people once predicted digital technology would remove all paper from office work. Instead, he pointed out, office jobs now use more paper in aggregate than ever before.

Here’s the teaser for the Unreal Engine Rogue Trooper movie

It’s directed by Duncan Jones, who directed the Warcraft movie, and stars Hayley Atwell, Aneurin Barnard, Jack Lowden, Jemaine Clement, Matt Berry, and Sean Bean. It’s based on the 2000 A.D. comic about genetic soldiers with minds uploaded into their gear and it was made entirely in Epic’s Unreal Engine 5.

Age verification comes to PlayStation

Insider Gaming reports that Sony plans to lock underage users out of messaging and chat features moving forward. It’s not yet clear how the new requirements will be implemented, but it follows similar rollouts in Fortnite and Roblox as regulators around the world double down on online child safety.

ICYMI:

  • Iron Galaxy was hit by new mass layoffs 
  • Did you miss the big surprise Switch 2 game sale over the weekend? 
  • Here’s every big movie announced at Hollywood’s E3 last week

Watch this:



https://kotaku.com/pragmata-sales-steam-charts-capcom-price-2000689091
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Elden Ring movie confirmed for release in 2028, directed by Alex Garland
item.content:encoded:
Bandai Namco Entertainment and movie production studio A24 have teamed up with Alex Garland to make a movie based on Elden Ring, with the release date set for March 3, 2028.

We also have a cast list with some big names signed up already, but we do not know what characters they will be playing.

  • Kit Connor (Warfare, Heartstopper)
  • Ben Whishaw (Skyfall, Paddington)
  • Cailee Spaeny (Alien: Romulus, Civil War)
  • Tom Burke (Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga, Black Bag)
  • Havana Rose Liu (Bottoms)
  • Sonoya Mizuno (Ex Machina)
  • Jonathan Pryce (The Two Popes)
  • Ruby Cruz (Willow, Bottoms)
  • Nick Offerman (The Last of Us)
  • John Hodgkinson
  • Jefferson Hall
  • Emma Laird
  • Peter Serafinowicz

Production will begin in Spring 2026, so like, now, and Alex Garland has both written and will direct the film. Garland is a well-respected movie producer and reportedly pitched the idea himself to Bandai Namco. He’s also a big gamer with many highly regarded movies under his belt, including Civil War (2024), Sunshine (2007), Ex Machina (2014) and Annihilation (2018).

According to The Hollywood Reporter, the film adaptation is set to have a budget well over $100 million, with around 100 days of principal photography planned, making it the most expensive film A24 has worked on.

News of the movie adaptation was announced over a year ago but things have been very quiet, but apparently moving at quite a pace.

Elden Ring was created under the guidance of FromSoftware’s Hidetaka Miyazaki, based on a mythological story written by George R. R. Martin, author of the fantasy novel series, “A Song of Ice and Fire.” That’s Game of Thrones if you’ve been living under a rock. The game has sold an astonishing 30 million copies worldwide as of April 2025, and spawned a new entry in the series with Elden Ring: Nightreign, which launched on 30th of May 2025.

Elden Ring’s dark and menacing world of brutal challenges is undoubtedly an evocative setting, and, if done well, could be a fantasy epic to rival Lord of the Rings, or Martin’s own Game of Thrones.

In 2024 Elden Ring director and FromSoftware president Hidetaka Miyazaki said, “We’re not really considering developments such as an Elden Ring 2,” but then acknowledged that they may work on the sequel in the future. He also suggested the franchise could continue in another form, such as a movie or animated series. Maybe he knew something back then!

Source: Bandai Namco



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/04/20/the-elden-ring-movie-confirmed-for-release-in-2028-directed-by-alex-garland/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Assassin’s Creed Black Flag Resynced showcase announced for this week
item.content:encoded:
Fair play to Ubisoft, the short trailer announcing the Assassin’s Creed Black Flag Resynced showcase begins with the words “Gaming’s worst kept secret”, a reference to the many leaks about the game before it was finally announced last month.

The showcase is dated for April 23rd at 4PM UTC / 6PM CEST  / 9AM PDT.  It is slightly odd for a remake of an old game to get an entire showcase to itself, but the rumours are that the game has changed quite a lot and now plays more like AC Valhalla.

According to Tom Henderson over on Insider Gaming the presentation will be 30 minutes long and the game will launch on July 9, 2026.

Black Flag Resynced has been hinted at and rumoured through much of last year. The voice actor for Edward Kenway dropped a hint last June, this was followed by rumours that the remake will drop the modern day sequences, and then it was discovered that the Black Flag Resynced domain was registered by a Ubisoft shell company.

The suggestion is that this will be a remake, rather than just a remaster, using the Anvil Pipeline engine, and it’s going to feature some fairly big changes. The most notable change will be the removal of all the modern-day sequences so we will not see Rebbeca, Shaun and the others. The modern-day plot, which worked really well in the first few games with Desmond, has gradually been side-lined and only appears as text in Assassin’s Creed Shadows. Replacing it will be “a few extra hours of content” including content that was cut from the original release, such as parts of Mary Read’s story.

Gameplay is said to be closer to the RPG style of the more recent games, with loot, stats for weapons, and a new inventory. The combat is is also said to lean more towards the style of the recent games. The map is the same size but will have more things to do, and modern systems mean there will be no loading screens when docking your ship and heading to land.

This is a pretty extensive project, in other words, with Ubisoft Singapore, Belgrade and Bordeaux reportedly working on it, and some assets from Skull and Bones possibly being reused to save costs. Fitting, of course, when Skull and Bones was spawned out of Black Flag in the first place.

Source: Ubisoft



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/04/20/assassins-creed-black-flag-resynced-showcase-date/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Minimo Interview: Can You Fit an MMO Into 30 Minutes?
item.content:encoded:
Emilien Lecoffre Updated: Apr 20, 2026 3:47 PM Posted: Apr 20, 2026 2:30 PM ET
Category: Interviews 0

Announced on March 25th and developed by Jon Selin and Mike Hines at their two-person Atlanta studio Low Drag Labs, Minimo describes itself as a "massively cooperative roguelike" - a genre label that doesn't exist yet. The pitch: 200 players drop into a procedurally generated world for 30-minute sessions, working together toward shared objectives that shift with every run. You could almost call it a Mini-MMO, and I find the concept genuinely intriguing given where the genre stands today. 

We've been asking ourselves lately what an MMO even is anymore, and in particular how much persistence matters to that definition. Minimo leans into that question head-on - it fully embraces the erosion of persistence, replacing it with replayability as a core design pillar. Each run is self-contained, and the roguelike structure turns what might feel like a limitation into a feature. 

The timing feels significant, too. We're reaching what feels like the end of an era for AAA MMORPGs, and with that comes the need for new funding models - which inevitably means new design models, likely built around smaller, leaner scopes. My conviction is that independent developers are well positioned to thrive in this landscape. And from what I've seen of Minimo while researching for this interview, it's a project with real ambition: it aims to revolutionize what an MMO can be, “by making them bite-sized”, and it brings genuinely compelling ideas to the table. We put our questions to Jon and Mike to dig into the details.

MMORPG: Minimo compresses what we could call “the full arc of an MMO” into 30 minutes - leveling, gearing, exploring, boss-killing. Could you walk us through a single run from drop to extraction: what does minute one look like versus minute twenty-five?

Jon Selin, co-founder Low Drag Labs: When a run starts, it feels like the starting gun of a fresh MMO server. Because of the roguelike structure, nobody knows exactly how things will play out - which areas will matter, which encounters will show up, or what builds will emerge. Players are experimenting early: swapping classes based on preference or group needs, forming loose comps, and spreading out to explore and gather resources.

By minute twenty-five, things have usually come together. You’ve got builds and synergies taking shape, much of the map has been uncovered, and the group is pushing into the later areas. That’s when the focus shifts to execution - taking on endgame bosses, sometimes ones you’ve never seen before, chasing unlocks like new classes, or trying to push a deeper run and beat your high score on the leaderboard.

You’ve announced 50 classes, including non-combat roles like chef, fisherman, and flower-picker. In a 30-minute run where every second counts, what stops a group of 200 from simply ignoring the non-combat masks and brute-forcing content with 200 Damage-dealers?

JS: You could do that, but it’s very sub-optimal.

The game is designed so that strong runs rely on a mix of roles. On the combat side, you need things like tanking, healing, different damage profiles, and buffs/debuffs to handle tougher encounters efficiently. But that extends beyond combat as well - activities like fishing, cooking, and mining are how you generate resources, unlock buffs, and enable stronger builds. Those systems have their own layers of optimization too. Different gathering roles, crafting, and even transporting resources all contribute to the overall power of the group.

The intent is that players naturally self-organize around those needs. Some focus on combat, others on enabling it, and the strongest runs come from how those pieces come together. And because each run is procedural - with different maps, encounters, and upgrade paths - the “right” composition shifts every time.

The democratic upgrade system has 200 players voting on shared power-ups. How does the voting actually work in practice? Is it majority rules, weighted by contribution, or something else? And what happens to the player who wanted a different build direction?

JS: At its core, this is a social game, and part of the gameplay is navigating that friction - convincing others, organizing, and making decisions under time pressure. Forming groups, building influence, and aligning people around a direction is as much a skill as combat or optimization.

Right now, it’s a simple majority vote. But we’re exploring alternatives,  for example, a weighted roll system has come up as an interesting idea, and it’s something we’d like to test.

You won’t always get the outcome you want, and that’s intentional. A big part of the experience is adapting - to the procedural world, to the upgrades you’re offered, and to the decisions the group makes. That uncertainty is what keeps each run feeling fresh, and the social layer plays a huge role in that.

You’ve built guild systems with customizable social spaces and leaderboards, but runs last 30 minutes and rosters can reach 200 strangers. How do you expect social bonds to form?

JS: A core part of MMOs is simply existing in a world with other players - those unscripted moments where you run into someone struggling with a miniboss, jump in to help, revive a downed player, or assist in getting resources back to base.

We’re designing Minimo around strong cooperative dependency. Players need to communicate and work together to solve problems, with inspiration from games like Overcooked and Helldivers, but at MMO scale.

When you create those meaningful, moment-to-moment interactions, social bonds form naturally. That’s what leads to players grouping up again, adding each other, and eventually joining guilds or community spaces outside the run.

Jon, you’ve participated in WoW world-first races and Elden Ring speedruns. Is Minimo designed to reward that kind of hardcore optimization?

JS: 100%. Getting the 2nd NA Ragnaros and wanting to democratize those moments was a big inspiration - those peak MMO experiences and organization around them usually only a tiny fraction of players ever get to see. They’re some of the most memorable moments in gaming, but they’re buried deep behind time, coordination, and commitment. We’re

With Minimo, we’re trying to bring that feeling to the surface. If you’re a hardcore player, there’s a lot to optimize - competing on leaderboards for boss kills, damage output, or fastest run times, and organizing large groups to push for world-record runs.

The April playtest is your first large-scale public test. What is the single most important question you need this playtest to answer? And what result would make you rethink a core design decision?

JS: We’re trying to create a new kind of game, and that’s a journey in stages. The first step was validating the idea - does this resonate at all? The overwhelming response to the trailer gave us a lot of confidence that we’re onto something. Now the next step is putting it in players’ hands and seeing how it actually plays.

This playtest marks the start of the next chapter: running structured playtests in waves, where players can give feedback and help shape the direction of the game. We’re looking to understand what’s resonating, where players are getting stuck, and what needs improvement or expansion.

Even with 25+ years in the industry, it’s still nerve-wracking and super exciting to run our first public playtest. There’s a lot for us to learn - even at a fundamental level - about how to run a successful playtest on our infrastructure.

Minimo has no announced business model yet. Given your decade in free-to-play mobile at Pocket Gems, Jon, how are you thinking about monetization ; and how do you reassure the MMO audience, which has been burned repeatedly by aggressive cash shops, that Minimo won’t follow that path?

JS: It’s too early to share exact details, but I’m happy to talk about the principles that will guide it.

Progression in Minimo is primarily horizontal - you’re unlocking new classes, cosmetics, and playstyles, not climbing a power curve. The goal is that players can jump in together regardless of time invested, and that every class - including starter classes - has a role at the highest level of content. A skilled new player should still be able to contribute meaningfully.

On a personal level, a big reason I went indie was to get away from the kind of design pressure where monetization starts driving every decision and takes over the entire development process. We’ll share specifics when we’re ready, but the philosophy is simple: gameplay comes first, progression stays fair, and we avoid creating power gaps where money determines success.

Your trademark filing covers PC, consoles, and mobile devices. Is Minimo being designed as a PC-first game with potential ports, or are you building for all platforms simultaneously? How does that choice affect the complexity of what you can put on screen with 200 players?

JS: Minimo is fundamentally a social game - the whole point is being able to invite your friends to jump in and play with you. And social games are only as strong as the number of people who can actually join. That’s why we’re designing with cross-platform accessibility in mind from the start. The goal is that you can pick up whatever device you have - iPad, Chromebook, PC - and get into a run together.

Right now, internally, we’re testing across PC, Mac, and WebGL, along with controller support. Mobile is next, and consoles will follow.

That approach has a big impact on how we build the game. Supporting 200 players means we have to be extremely disciplined about performance and readability. Everything needs to be clear, efficient, and scalable across a wide range of hardware, and our tech stack decisions have been shaped around that from day one.

Jon, your career path goes MUDs → Dreamlords at Lockpick → World of Darkness at CCP → a decade of mobile at Pocket Gems. Mike, you’ve shipped art at Perpetual, Epic, and Oculus. What specifically did each of those experiences teach you that you’re applying directly to Minimo’s design?

JS: I’ve always been drawn to creating new types of experiences - whether that’s working on new platforms, new genres, or finding different ways for players to interact.

The throughline across everything I’ve worked on is social gameplay. Real collaboration, where players rely on each other and form relationships through shared experiences. That shows up in a lot of ways: giving players different roles and playstyles, making sure everyone can contribute regardless of experience level, and creating systems where groups become stronger than the sum of their parts.

There’s also a strong focus on exploration and discovery - unlocking new abilities, experimenting with different builds, and finding your own path through the game. And just as importantly, building something that’s endlessly replayable, where each session feels like its own unique journey.

Some people are surprised by aspects of Minimo given my background with darker, more PvP-focused games, but those were driven by the same core ideas. This is just a different expression of them - with cooperation and large-scale social play front and center.

Mike Hines, co-founder Low Drag Labs: A big part of my work has always been figuring out how to build experiences that scale, both technically and visually.

On the art side, that means leaning into stylization and a strong visual language. When you have a lot happening on screen, like 200 players at once, clarity becomes essential. Every class, ability, and threat needs to be instantly readable so players can make quick decisions. On the technical side, it means building systems that can support that scale across a wide range of platforms. That intersection of gameplay needs and technical constraints is a big part of what shaped Minimo’s look.

I’ve also always been focused on creating worlds that feel varied and alive. Different biomes, encounters, and environmental storytelling help make each run feel distinct, even within a structured session.

And a big part of the work has been building tools and pipelines that let a small team move quickly. For a game like Minimo, where replayability and variation are core, being able to generate, tune, and iterate on content efficiently is just as important as the content itself. All of that comes together in Minimo. It has to stay readable, performant, and scalable.

A Smaller Scale for a Bigger Future?

One thing that stands out from this conversation is how aware Jon and Mike are of the technical constraints surrounding their project. For an online game - especially one targeting 200 concurrent players - that's essential, and the seriousness with which they approach it is reassuring. Another takeaway, one that I personally find less exciting but understand the reasoning behind, is the cross-platform ambition, and particularly the push toward mobile. 

This echoes what other veteran developers have been saying lately. Greg Street, Rich Vogel, and Scott Hartsman, in a WCCFTech interview from a few months ago reflecting on the state of the MMO genre after the wave of high-profile cancellations in late 2025 and early 2026, all made a similar case: the future calls for humbler, more flexible game designs - projects that can grow over time, scale sustainably, and, crucially, actually get funded. In short, making MMO-scale projects more accessible and more reasonable, given the climate surrounding AAA MMORPGs today. 

That funding reality naturally extends to platform availability: reaching as many potential players as possible may not be just a growth strategy, but a survival one. As a longtime PC MMORPG player, I can't say this thrills me - cross-platform development inevitably means technical compromise - and I suspect many readers with a similar profile feel the same way. But given the crisis the genre is going through, it's a bet that makes sense (though only time will tell if it proves a necessity for the genre).

On the monetization front, while Jon didn't share specifics, the philosophy he outlined is encouraging. His emphasis on horizontal progression - where unlocking new classes and playstyles takes priority over climbing a power curve - suggests a genuine commitment to fairness. Reading their answers, I came away with the impression that their goal is to build a game that is not only technically and financially viable, but equitable for every type of player. I'm eager to see how it plays out. I believe we're at a turning point for the MMORPG genre, and projects like Minimo will play a central role in shaping what we consider an MMO - or a mini-MMO - tomorrow. The game's first public playtest is scheduled for April, and if you're as intrigued as I am, you can sign up for access.

Emilien Lecoffre

Emilien 'Nephistos' has been immersed in MMOs since his early teens on Dofus. Over the past years, he also has been sharing his insights on JeuxOnline, a major French-language MMO sites. While he keeps a keen eye on all market offerings, his true passion lies in RvR and mass PvP.



https://www.mmorpg.com/interviews/minimo-interview-can-you-fit-an-mmo-into-30-minutes-2000137851
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Counter-Strike Player Sucker-Punches His Opponent After Losing And Gets A 10-Year Ban
item.content:encoded:
A Counter-Strike 2 pro named MAUschine has been officially banned from competing in any further eSports events for 10 years after punching one of his competitors in the face, live on stage, following his loss at the CAGGTUS Leipzig tournament last night.

The altercation took place on April 19 during the livestreamed tournament during the closing awards segment. As the clips circulating on social media show, MAUschine brazenly struck competitor Spidergum in the face while the pros were lining up to accept their awards at the end of the CS2 event.

A counter strike player known as MAUschine has been handed a 10 year ban after sucker punching a member of the winning team pic.twitter.com/1GEmvv85m2

— hobshy (@hobshy) April 19, 2026

While there’s obviously no justification for the assault, users on X think they’ve discovered what led to MAUschine’s decision to punch his opponent. As shown in the clip below, Spidergum shouted the phrase “papi chulo,” which is apparently MAUschine’s catchphrase, after killing MAUschine during the tournament. It’s also Spanish for “cute daddy,” if that’s in any way relevant.

VIDEO Showing his Reasoning behind the Punch 😭🤦‍♂️ pic.twitter.com/VB6TwayOWA

— ClipGD (@ClipGD) April 19, 2026

MAUschine was immediately escorted offstage following the assault and, as spotted by Dot eSports, was subsequently issued a 10-year-long ban by event organizers DACH CS Masters.

“We do not tolerate physical assaults against other players at LAN and have acted accordingly,” DACH CS Masters announced in a post on X. “MAUschine is banned for at least 10 years and the incident has additionally been reported to [Esports Integrity Commission]. In case it wasn’t clear, we think violence has no place in our league.”

Spidergum seems to be unshaken by the assault, as he played it off with a tongue-in-cheek post on X following the event. “Sick, experienced everything,” Spidergum stated in a post on X (as machin- translated from German), “B3B Caggtus CS2 winner. Bullseye from Mauschine, better aim than with the AWP and especially on stream.”

However, based on his reply to a comment from someone on his post, who stated that he should “consider reporting that guy,” Spidergum is seemingly considering following up on the assault, as he simply replied, “Yes, you’re right.”



https://kotaku.com/counter-strike-2-mauschine-spidergum-caggtus-2000689094
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: You Can Now Play League of Legends With a Controller
item.content:encoded:
After 17 years, League of Legends finally supports game controllers thanks to the addition of WASD controls to the MOBA.

Riot Games has added WASD controls in ranked to make LoL more appealing to new players.

And with that update has come various accessibility improvements to LoL, including the ability to move the mouse cursor with custom inputs.

Along with all these changes, LoL players can now play the game on the live servers using Xbox or PlayStation controllers without having to make any other settings adjustments.

As long as WASD is enabled, players can plug in and use a controller with a built-in control scheme instead of a keyboard and mouse.

The following Xbox controls for League of Legends are:

  • LT: Q
  • LB: W
  • RB: E
  • RT: R
  • X: D
  • Y: F
  • A: Auto Attack
  • D-Pad Down: Trinket
  • Analog stick: mouse and champion movement

Moving the left stick will move the champion around, while the right stick moves the cursor to aim abilities and buy items in the shop.

There are no official settings for controllers in LoL as of yet, but content creator SkinSpotlights believes it will be added eventually after looking into the game’s files.

However, Riot claims this is not the case, and joystick support was only added as an accessibility feature, stating:

“Something we heard from a lot of folks was the desire to use WASD on a joystick. While we don’t have plans to officially support controllers or joysticks broadly right now, we did want to enable play on joysticks through remapping of WASD for accessibility reasons.”

League of Legends was supposed to become playable on console through the mobile version, Wild Rift.

However, Riot scrapped plans to release a console version and revealed that choice back in 2024.

Wild Rift executive David ‘Papa Smoothie’ Xu explained:

“I wanted to officially let y’all know that we’re not pursuing a console launch at this moment.

“This was a deliberate decision to prioritize mobile investments over maintaining and trying to balance development between mobile and consoles.

“Our goal is to bring the right games to the right platforms for players, so while we see games like VAL or 2KXO show up on consoles because of the existing popularity of those genres on the platform, we haven’t seen the same for the MOBA genre.

“Splitting our dev team’s focus between console and mobile wasn’t a tradeoff we felt was right for WR players right now.”

Would you play a console version of LoL? Let us know on the Insider Gaming Discord.

And in another crazy addition to the game, the reworked Statikk Shiv is too broken to be released.

MOBILE APP

Level Up Your Gaming News

Real-time news, exclusive podcasts, push alerts, and a better reading experience.

Available on iOS & Android



https://insider-gaming.com/you-can-now-play-league-of-legends-with-a-controller/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Blue Protocol: Star Resonance Confirms Next Month's Mystery Collaboration
item.content:encoded:
Blue Protocol: Star Resonance has revealed its third of three promised anime collaborations. Last month, the team announced collaborations with Shangri-La Frontier ending this week) and Fairy-Tail, but left the third collaboration a mystery.

The third collaboration is with Monogatari Series and is coming in May, following the Fairy-Tail collab. Monogatari features a high school student named Koyomi Araragi, who has been cured of vampirism. Now that he’s human again, he decides to try and help others who have been affected by oddities, usually supernatural in origin, like ghosts and spirits. 

An anime adaptation, was based on a series of light novels, and began airing back in 2009 in Japan. While there’s no info just yet about what this collaboration with the anime-inspired MMORPG will entail, the Shangri-La Frontier campaign had special cosmetics and furniture items, as well as other items coming out throughout the month-long event.

Next month, the Fairy Tail collaboration will feature characters from the Wizard Guild as part of the crossover event. After that collab ends, we can expect the Monogatari event. 

Today is April 20th, so with May just around the corner, we should be hearing lots more info and getting details about both of these collaborations starting pretty soon. 



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/blue-protocol-star-resonance-confirms-next-months-mystery-collaboration-2000137850
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: A Surprise Nintendo Pop-Up Store Has Arrived In London
item.content:encoded:
Tottenham Court Toad

After years of feeling left out, it feels like London is being treated to a bit of Nintendo store recognition, of late. First, we had the Westfield pop-up at the end of last year, and now there's another surprise store in town, taking up floor space in an Argos, of all places.

Out of nowhere, the Tottenham Court Argos is now home to a bunch of physical Nintendo merch, covering everything from plushies to games, Switch 2 consoles to accessories. Heck, there are even a few items that you usually only see online — hey Alarmo, Talking Flower and amiibo!

Being the local lad that this writer is, we popped up to the store to see what it's all about. It's got to be said, we were impressed by just how much merch is on display, particularly since Argos stores are usually nothing more than a sea of screens (we miss you, catalogues).

Here's a quick look at what's on offer, because it's always good to go in with a list at these kinds of things:

Let's give a little shoutout to the lesser-represented Nintendo franchises, too. It may not be much, but it's always exciting to see Pikmin and Metroid on the shelf!

A store employee told us that the pop-up will be sticking around for a while yet, so be sure to swing by if you happen to find yourself in the capital and have a hankering for some Nintendo merch.

Will you be heading to this Nintendo pop-up any time soon? Let us know in the comments.

Jim came to Nintendo Life in 2022 and, despite his insistence that The Minish Cap is the best Zelda game and his unwavering love for the Star Wars prequels (yes, really), he has continued to write news and features on the site ever since.

  • Author Profile
  • Bluesky
  • Reply


https://www.nintendolife.com/news/2026/04/a-surprise-nintendo-pop-up-store-has-arrived-in-london
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Oscar Isaac Tried Really Hard To Make Star Wars Episode 9 Palpatine Line Not Sound Dumb
item.content:encoded:
Remember that part of Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker when a tired-sounding Poe Dameron tells everyone that “Somehow, Palpatine has returned.” It’s gone on to become a popular meme, which the actor who said it, Oscar Isaac, didn’t anticipate. He also seemed to suggest that, yeah, he thought it was terrible, too.

Released in 2019, Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker is the third film in the sequel trilogy and the last main entry (for now…) in the saga. It isn’t a great movie. One of its biggest problems is that it brings back Emperor Palpatine, via a plot point that played out in Fortnite (really), and has Isaac’s Poe Dameron announce this news to everyone with that now infamously bad line. According to the actor, that bit of dialogue was added at the last minute during a messy production to help tie the film’s story together.

When asked about the line during a recent episode of Josh Horowitz’s podcast, Isaac explained that the “Somehow” line was added during a last-minute round of Rise of Skywalker reshoots.

“Those were reshoots,” said Isaac. “We had to do reshoots. Yeah, because when I look at it, I’m like, ‘Oh, that wig’s pretty good.’ Y’know, I think I’d cut my hair already.”

Isaac further explained that lines and reshoots like that are “surgical strikes” where a director and everyone involved come in and try to “get everything” to come together and work. And on the set of Rise of Skywalker, Isaac says there was a lot of “movement and flux throughout that whole thing.” Still, he didn’t expect the line to become infamous.

“But had you asked me if, at the moment, I thought that [line] was going to be [a meme], I wouldn’t have known,” said Isaac.

He added, “Hey, man, I committed to the exasperation, that’s for sure,” before laughing. So yeah, it seems clear that while he didn’t know at the time the line would become a meme, he did realize how silly and bad it was to shove it into the movie like this. Even Isaac was fed up with Rise of Skywalker at that point.

Oh, and if you were wondering if the Poe Dameron actor had any involvement in the now-canceled Kylo Ren film from Steven Soderbergh, he says he never got to see the script, as he wasn’t involved. But when asked to share his thoughts on a Soderbergh-directed Star Wars movie, Isaac replied:  “That would have been amazing, sure. Would have been incredible.”



https://kotaku.com/oscar-isaac-tried-really-hard-to-make-star-wars-episode-9-palpatine-line-not-sound-dum-2000689083
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
